content:
# Leviticus Chapter 26 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 74r–74v | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 112–113 | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** "You shall not make for yourselves idols, nor shall you erect a carved image or a pillar for yourselves, nor shall you set up a figured stone in your land to bow down to it, for I am the LORD your God.

**2** You shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary: I am the LORD.

**3** "If you walk in my statutes and observe my commandments and do them,

**4** then I will give your rains in their season, and the land shall yield its produce, and the trees of the field shall yield their fruit.

**5** Your threshing shall last to the time of the grape harvest, and the grape harvest shall last to the time of sowing. And you shall eat your bread to the full and dwell in your land securely.

**6** I will give peace in the land, and you shall lie down, and none shall make you afraid. And I will remove harmful beasts from the land, and the sword shall not go through your land.

**7** You shall chase your enemies, and they shall fall before you by the sword.

**8** Five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand, and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword.

**9** I will turn to you and make you fruitful and multiply you and will confirm my covenant with you.

**10** You shall eat old grain long kept, and you shall clear out the old to make way for the new.

**11** I will make my dwelling among you, and my soul shall not abhor you.

**12** And I will walk among you and will be your God, and you shall be my people.

**13** I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, that you should not be their slaves. And I have broken the bars of your yoke and made you walk erect.

**14** "But if you will not listen to me and will not do all these commandments,

**15** if you spurn my statutes, and if your soul abhors my rules, so that you will not do all my commandments, and so break my covenant,

**16** then I will do this to you: I will visit you with panic, with wasting disease and fever that consume the eyes and make the heart ache. And you shall sow your seed in vain, for your enemies shall eat it.

**17** I will set my face against you, and you shall be struck down before your enemies. Those who hate you shall rule over you, and you shall flee when none pursues you.

**18** And if in spite of this you will not listen to me, then I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins,

**19** and I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your heavens like iron and your earth like bronze.

**20** And your strength shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield its produce, and the trees of the land shall not yield their fruit.

**21** "Then if you walk contrary to me and will not listen to me, I will continue striking you, sevenfold for your sins.

**22** And I will let loose the wild beasts against you, which shall bereave you of your children and destroy your livestock and make you few in number, so that your roads shall be deserted.

**23** "And if by these things you are not turned to me but walk contrary to me,

**24** then I also will walk contrary to you, and I myself will strike you sevenfold for your sins.

**25** And I will bring a sword upon you, that shall execute vengeance for the covenant. And if you gather within your cities, I will send pestilence among you, and you shall be delivered into the hand of the enemy.

**26** When I break your supply of bread, ten women shall bake your bread in a single oven and shall dole out your bread again by weight, and you shall eat and not be satisfied.

**27** "But if in spite of this you will not listen to me, but walk contrary to me,

**28** then I will walk contrary to you in fury, and I myself will discipline you sevenfold for your sins.

**29** You shall eat the flesh of your sons, and you shall eat the flesh of your daughters.

**30** And I will destroy your high places and cut down your incense altars and cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols, and my soul shall abhor you.

**31** And I will lay your cities waste and will make your sanctuaries desolate, and I will not smell your pleasing aromas.

**32** And I myself will devastate the land, so that your enemies who settle in it shall be appalled at it.

**33** And I will scatter you among the nations, and I will unsheathe the sword after you, and your land shall be a desolation, and your cities shall be a waste.

**34** "Then the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths as long as it lies desolate, while you are in your enemies' land; then the land shall rest and enjoy its Sabbaths.

**35** As long as it lies desolate it shall have rest, the rest that it did not have on your Sabbaths when you were dwelling in it.

**36** And as for those of you who are left, I will send faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight, and they shall flee as one flees from the sword, and they shall fall when none pursues.

**37** They shall stumble over one another, as if to escape a sword, though none pursues. And you shall have no power to stand before your enemies.

**38** And you shall perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies shall eat you up.

**39** And those of you who are left shall rot away in your enemies' lands because of their iniquity, and also because of the iniquities of their fathers they shall rot away like them.

**40** "But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery that they committed against me, and also in walking contrary to me,

**41** so that I walked contrary to them and brought them into the land of their enemies — if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled and they make amends for their iniquity,

**42** then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land.

**43** But the land shall be abandoned by them and enjoy its Sabbaths while it lies desolate without them, and they shall make amends for their iniquity, because they spurned my rules and their soul abhorred my statutes.

**44** Yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them, for I am the LORD their God.

**45** But I will for their sake remember the covenant with their forefathers, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, that I might be their God: I am the LORD."

**46** These are the statutes and rules and laws that the LORD made between himself and the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai through Moses.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Idols" (v. 1):** Hebrew אֱלִילִים, from a root meaning "worthless, of no account." The word is a deliberately diminutive term for foreign gods — not the neutral אֱלֹהִים ("gods") but the polemical אֱלִילִים, communicating contempt for what others worship. LOGOS renders "idols" following the ESV/NASB tradition. The LXX renders εἴδωλα ("images/idols"), the standard Greek polemical term for false gods.

2. **"Carved image" (v. 1):** Hebrew פֶּסֶל, from פָּסַל ("to hew, to carve"). The term refers specifically to a sculpted or carved image, as opposed to a cast or molded one (מַסֵּכָה). The Decalogue forbids both (Exod 20:4). The singular "carved image" here broadens the first prohibition: not just the idols in general but specifically the three-dimensional cultic image. The LXX renders γλυπτόν ("carved/sculpted thing").

3. **"Pillar" (v. 1):** Hebrew מַצֵּבָה, a standing stone used as a cultic monument. In earlier patriarchal narratives the massebah was acceptable (Gen 28:18; 31:45), but later legislation consistently prohibits it (Exod 23:24; Deut 16:22). LOGOS renders "pillar" as the standard formal equivalence rendering. Per project convention (from Genesis), massebah could be preserved with a bracketed gloss, but "pillar" is standard in Leviticus context. The LXX renders στήλη ("stele/monument").

4. **"Figured stone" (v. 1):** Hebrew אֶבֶן מַשְׂכִּית, lit. "stone of imagery/design." The מַשְׂכִּית is a stone with carved or painted images used for prostration in worship — a cult pavement or cultic stone installation. It appears only here and in Num 33:52 and Ezek 8:12. LOGOS renders "figured stone" following the ESV; NASB renders "figured stone" as well. The LXX renders λίθον σκοπόν ("a stone for gazing/vision"), possibly understanding it as a stone used in divination by gazing.

5. **"Reverence my sanctuary" (v. 2):** Hebrew וּמִקְדָּשִׁי תִּירָאוּ, lit. "and my sanctuary you shall fear." The verb יָרֵא ("to fear, to reverence") governs both Sabbath observance and sanctuary respect. The juxtaposition of "keep my Sabbaths" with "reverence my sanctuary" connects time and space as the two holy dimensions of covenant life. The LXX renders τὰ ἅγιά μου φοβηθήσεσθε ("you shall fear my holy things").

6. **"Walk in my statutes" (v. 3):** Hebrew בְּחֻקֹּתַי תֵּלֵכוּ, lit. "in my statutes you shall walk." The verb הָלַךְ ("to walk, to go") is the standard metaphor for a life-pattern or moral direction — "walking" in a way, following a path. Chuqqot (חֻקֹּת) are the LORD's "statutes," the category of divine commands whose reasons may not be stated but whose authority is absolute. LOGOS renders "walk in my statutes" preserving the Hebraic metaphor. The LXX renders ἐν τοῖς νόμοις μου πορεύησθε ("walk in my laws").

7. **"Rains in their season" (v. 4):** Hebrew גִּשְׁמֵיכֶם בְּעִתָּם, lit. "your rains in their time." The plural גְּשָׁמִים ("rains") refers to the seasonal rain pattern of Canaan — the early rains (יוֹרֶה, autumn) and the late rains (מַלְקוֹשׁ, spring) — both essential for agriculture in the Levant. Rain "in its time" is the paradigmatic blessing; drought "in its time" the paradigmatic curse (v. 19). The LXX renders ὑετοὺς ὑμῖν ἐν καιρῷ αὐτῶν ("rains to you in their season").

8. **"Your threshing shall last to the time of the grape harvest" (v. 5):** Hebrew וְהִשִּׂיג לָכֶם דַּיִשׁ אֶת-בָּצִיר. The verb נָשַׂג (here Hiphil, "to reach, to overtake") conveys overlapping abundance — the grain threshing extends so long it runs into the grape harvest season, which itself extends into the sowing season. The three agricultural seasons crowd one another in a picture of overflowing productivity. The LXX renders καταλήμψεται ὑμᾶς ὁ ἀλοητὸς τὸν τρύγητον ("the threshing shall overtake the vintage for you").

9. **"Lie down, and none shall make you afraid" (v. 6):** Hebrew וּשְׁכַבְתֶּם וְאֵין מַחֲרִיד, lit. "and you shall lie down with none causing trembling." The Hiphil participle מַחֲרִיד ("one who causes to tremble/startle") is a vivid image: the deeply peaceful sleep of those with no threat. The same phrase appears in Micah 4:4 and Zeph 3:13 in eschatological visions of final shalom. The LXX renders κοιμηθήσεσθε καὶ οὐκ ἔσται ὁ ἐκφοβῶν ὑμᾶς ("you shall sleep and none shall terrify you").

10. **"Harmful beasts" (v. 6):** Hebrew חַיָּה רָעָה, lit. "evil/bad animal." The term is generic for dangerous wild animals — lions, bears, wolves — that prey on livestock and humans. The removal of such animals from the land is a blessing of security paralleled in Ezek 34:25 in the "covenant of peace." The LXX renders θηρία πονηρά ("evil/harmful wild animals").

11. **"I will make you fruitful and multiply you" (v. 9):** Hebrew וְהִפְרֵיתִי אֶתְכֶם וְהִרְבֵּיתִי אֶתְכֶם, the two verbs פָּרָה ("to be fruitful") and רָבָה ("to multiply") in the Hiphil. These are the same two verbs from the creation mandate (Gen 1:28) and the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 17:2, 6). Their appearance here confirms that covenant obedience realizes the original creation blessing — the covenant is the instrument of creation's fulfillment. The LXX renders αὐξανῶ ὑμᾶς καὶ πληθυνῶ ὑμᾶς ("I will increase you and multiply you").

12. **"Confirm my covenant with you" (v. 9):** Hebrew וַהֲקִמֹתִי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם, the Hiphil of קוּם ("to stand, to rise") — "I will cause my covenant to stand." The covenant is not merely remembered or maintained but actively upheld/established/confirmed. This same verb is used in God's covenant with Noah (Gen 6:18; 9:9, 11) and with Abraham (Gen 17:7). The LXX renders στήσω τὴν διαθήκην μου μεθ' ὑμῶν ("I will establish my covenant with you").

13. **"Old grain long kept" (v. 10):** Hebrew יָשָׁן נוֹשָׁן, the doubled adjective from יָשַׁן ("to be old") — "old, very old." The blessing is so abundant that stored grain from previous years still fills the storehouses when the new harvest comes in. The image is one of superabundance: not the bare minimum but enough to age. The phrase is unique in the MT. The LXX renders παλαιὰ παλαιῶν ("old things of old things"), preserving the doubled form.

14. **"I will make my dwelling among you" (v. 11):** Hebrew וְנָתַתִּי מִשְׁכָּנִי בְּתוֹכְכֶם, lit. "I will give/place my dwelling-place in your midst." The מִשְׁכָּן ("dwelling-place, tabernacle") is here not only the physical tent-structure but God's own dwelling presence. This verse is a direct allusion to the tabernacle theology of Exodus 25–40, where God promises to "dwell among" (שָׁכַן בְּתוֹךְ) his people. The promise recurs in amplified form in Ezek 37:26–27 and Rev 21:3. The LXX renders θήσω τὴν σκηνήν μου ἐν ὑμῖν ("I will place my tabernacle among you").

15. **"My soul shall not abhor you" (vv. 11, 30):** Hebrew וְלֹא-תִגְעַל נַפְשִׁי אֶתְכֶם. The verb גָּעַל ("to abhor, to reject with disgust") is an emotionally strong term. That God speaks of his own "soul" (נֶפֶשׁ) rejecting Israel uses the full anthropomorphic language of divine pathos — the opposite of the intimacy promised in vv. 11–12. In v. 30 the same phrase appears as the curse correlate: covenant violation does what divine mercy prevents. The LXX renders οὐ βδελύξεται ἡ ψυχή μου ὑμᾶς ("my soul shall not abominate you").

16. **"I will walk among you" (v. 12):** Hebrew וְהִתְהַלַּכְתִּי בְּתוֹכְכֶם, the Hithpael ("walk about among, wander through") of הָלַךְ. This is the most intimate divine-presence language possible — the same form used in Gen 3:8 of God "walking about" in the garden. The Hithpael suggests ongoing, habitual movement through Israel's midst, not a static indwelling. The LXX renders ἐμπεριπατήσω ἐν ὑμῖν ("I will walk about among you"), with the compound ἐμπεριπατέω preserving the thoroughness of the traversal.

17. **"Bars of your yoke" (v. 13):** Hebrew מֹטֹת עֻלְּכֶם, the wooden crosspiece (מוֹט) of a yoke-frame. The "bar" is the horizontal piece that rests on the neck of the draft animal; breaking it removes the yoke entirely. The Exodus is re-interpreted here not just as political liberation but as the removal of the degrading submission-posture ("made you walk erect," קוֹמְמִיּוּת). The LXX renders τὸν δεσμὸν τοῦ ζυγοῦ ὑμῶν ("the bond/fastening of your yoke").

18. **"Walk erect" (v. 13):** Hebrew קוֹמְמִיּוּת, a hapax legomenon meaning "uprightness, erect posture." Rendered literally, it captures the image of a person who had been bowed under a yoke now standing tall — a bodily image of liberation. The LXX renders ὀρθούς ("upright/straight"), preserving the posture-language.

19. **"Panic" (v. 16):** Hebrew בֶּהָלָה, sudden overwhelming terror. The first curse in the five-stage escalation is psychological — fear and panic striking before any physical blow. The LXX renders ἀπορίαν ("perplexity/helplessness"), which captures the disorientation of panic without the terror element of the Hebrew.

20. **"Wasting disease and fever" (v. 16):** Hebrew שַׁחֶפֶת וְאֶת-הַקַּדַּחַת. The שַׁחֶפֶת ("wasting disease, consumption") and קַדַּחַת ("burning fever, malaria-type fever") are both difficult to identify with precision. Together they represent the physical deterioration that follows panic. The same pair appears in Deut 28:22. LOGOS renders "wasting disease and fever" following the ESV. The LXX renders τὴν ἀπορίαν, τό τε θερμάσιον ("helplessness and the burning/fever").

21. **"Consume the eyes and make the heart ache" (v. 16):** Hebrew מְכַלּוֹת עֵינַיִם וּמְדִיבֹת נָפֶשׁ, lit. "consuming the eyes and causing the soul to pine away." The two participles describe the bodily and inner effects of the disease: the eyes grow dim or fail, and the נֶפֶשׁ ("life, soul") pines away in grief and weakness. The LXX renders ἐκτήκουσα ὀφθαλμοὺς καὶ καταναλίσκουσα ψυχήν ("melting the eyes and consuming the soul").

22. **"You shall sow your seed in vain" (v. 16):** Hebrew וּזְרַעְתֶּם לָרִיק זַרְעֲכֶם. The "in vain" (לָרִיק, "for emptiness") is the reversal of the blessing in vv. 4–5. The labor of planting produces nothing — or rather, it produces a harvest that others consume (enemies). The futility curse is a standard ancient Near Eastern covenant curse motif, attested in Hittite treaty texts. The LXX renders σπερεῖτε τὰ σπέρματα ὑμῶν κενά ("you shall sow your seeds as empty/vain things").

23. **"Walk contrary to me" (vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41):** Hebrew קֶרִי / בְּקֶרִי, the key term of the curse section. The noun קֶרִי appears only in Leviticus 26 (seven times: vv. 21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 40, 41). Its root is disputed — possibly from קָרָה ("to encounter, to happen by chance") yielding "randomness, hostility, indifference," or from a root meaning "opposition, contrariness." The dominant interpretation is "hostile/contrary opposition." The LXX consistently renders πλάγιος or related terms ("oblique, perverse"), or with the full phrase κατ' ἐναντίον μου ("against me"). LOGOS renders "contrary to me" throughout, following the ESV and NASB, which preserves the repetition and the legal flavor.

24. **"Sevenfold for your sins" (vv. 18, 21, 24, 28):** Hebrew שֶׁבַע עַל-חַטֹּאתֵיכֶם. The fourfold repetition of "sevenfold" (שֶׁבַע, "seven") marks the five-stage escalation of curses, with each stage designated as "seven times" more intense. Seven as the number of completeness means "full, complete measure" — not arithmetic multiplication but totality of covenant consequences. The LXX renders ἑπτάκις ("seven times") each occurrence.

25. **"Break the pride of your power" (v. 19):** Hebrew וְשָׁבַרְתִּי אֶת-גְּאוֹן עֻזְּכֶם. The גָּאוֹן ("majesty, pride, swelling") of עֹז ("strength, power") — the confident, expansive self-reliance that prosperity produces — is specifically targeted. The curse does not merely remove strength but breaks its pride, its self-conception. The LXX renders συντρίψω τὴν ὕβριν τῆς ὑπερηφανίας ὑμῶν ("I will break the insolence of your pride/arrogance").

26. **"Heavens like iron and your earth like bronze" (v. 19):** Hebrew שָׁמַיִם כַּבַּרְזֶל וְאֶת-אַרְצְכֶם כַּנְּחֻשָׁה. The image is of a sky with no rain (hard, unyielding as iron) and ground with no moisture (hard, unyielding as bronze). The metals symbolize impenetrability — neither sky nor earth responds. Deut 28:23 reverses the order ("your heavens above shall be bronze and the earth beneath you shall be iron"), possibly for literary variation. Both formulations describe absolute drought. The LXX renders τὸν οὐρανὸν ὑμῖν σιδηροῦν καὶ τὴν γῆν ὑμῶν ὡσεὶ χαλκῆν ("your heaven as iron and your earth like bronze").

27. **"Wild beasts" (v. 22):** Hebrew חַיַּת הַשָּׂדֶה, lit. "animals of the field." The second-stage curse intensifies beyond the removal of peace (v. 6) to active predation by wild creatures. The population collapse from disease, famine, and animal attack leaves the roads "deserted" — a haunting image of the reversal of a thriving civilization. The LXX renders τὰ θηρία τὰ ἄγρια ("the wild/savage animals of the field").

28. **"Execute vengeance for the covenant" (v. 25):** Hebrew נֹקֶמֶת נְקַם-בְּרִית. The "vengeance of the covenant" (נְקַם בְּרִית) is a remarkable phrase: the sword is personified as the instrument that enforces covenant stipulations — breach of covenant triggers the covenant's own punitive mechanism. The LXX renders ἐκδικοῦσαν δίκην διαθήκης ("executing the punishment/judgment of the covenant").

29. **"Break your supply of bread" (v. 26):** Hebrew בִּשְׁבָרִי לָכֶם מַטֵּה-לֶחֶם, lit. "when I break for you the staff of bread." The "staff of bread" (מַטֵּה לֶחֶם) is the metaphorical "support-staff" of the food supply — as a walking staff supports the body, bread supports life. To "break the staff of bread" is to withdraw the sustaining resource of civilized existence. The same image appears in Ezek 4:16; 5:16; 14:13; Ps 105:16. The LXX renders συντρίψω τὴν σκῆπτρον τοῦ ἄρτου ὑμῶν ("I will break the scepter/staff of your bread").

30. **"Ten women shall bake your bread in a single oven" (v. 26):** A vivid image of scarcity: normally each household had its own oven, and one woman could bake for a family. Severe famine reduces the food supply so drastically that ten women share a single oven — and even then "dole out bread by weight" (rationing). The weight-rationing of bread is the ultimate sign of famine conditions. The LXX preserves the detail faithfully: δέκα γυναῖκες ἄρτους ὑμῶν ἐν κλιβάνῳ ἑνί ("ten women shall bake your bread in a single oven").

31. **"Your high places" (v. 30):** Hebrew בָּמֹתֵיכֶם, the cult platforms and outdoor worship sites that Israel shared with Canaanite religious practice. The destruction of Israel's own high places as divine judgment is ironic: the LORD removes the illegitimate worship sites as punishment. The LXX renders τὰ ὑψηλά ὑμῶν ("your high things/places").

32. **"Incense altars" (v. 30):** Hebrew חַמָּנֵיכֶם, from חַמָּן — stone or wooden pillars associated with sun worship (from חַמָּה, "sun"). These are distinct from the Mosaic altar and represent the syncretistic cult installations Israel was forbidden to use. LOGOS renders "incense altars" following the ESV/NASB; the term likely refers to incense stands associated with pagan solar worship. The LXX renders τὰ εἴδωλα ὑμῶν ("your idols"), generalizing the specific term.

33. **"Cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols" (v. 30):** Hebrew וְנָתַתִּי אֶת-פִּגְרֵיכֶם עַל-פִּגְרֵי גִּלּוּלֵיכֶם. The פֶּגֶר ("corpse") of the Israelites cast upon the פֶּגֶר of their גִּלּוּלִים ("idols/dung-idols") — the word גִּלּוּל is a pejorative for idols, derived from גָּלַל ("dung/rolling things"), conveying contempt. The image is grotesque: worshipers lying dead atop the lifeless objects of their worship. The LXX renders τὰ κῶλα ὑμῶν ἐπὶ τὰ κῶλα τῶν εἰδώλων ὑμῶν ("your limbs upon the limbs of your idols").

34. **"Sanctuaries desolate" (v. 31):** Hebrew מִקְדְּשֵׁיכֶם, the plural. The word normally refers to the single tabernacle/temple (מִקְדָּשׁ), but the plural may here polemically include all the unauthorized local shrines Israel operated, or may use the plural as an intensive for the one sanctuary. The LXX renders τὰ ἅγια ὑμῶν ("your holy places"), preserving the plural.

35. **"I will not smell your pleasing aromas" (v. 31):** Hebrew וְלֹא אָרִיחַ בְּרֵיחַ נִיחֹחֲכֶם. The LORD's smelling of sacrificial aromas (רֵיחַ נִיחוֹחַ, "aroma of soothing/restfulness") is the standard Levitical formula for divine acceptance of sacrifice (Lev 1:9, 13, 17, etc.). The explicit reversal here — "I will not smell" — means the sacrificial system has been rendered inoperative by sin. The desolation of the sanctuary makes sacrifice impossible; even if attempted, it would not be received. The LXX renders καὶ οὐ μὴ ὀσφρανθῶ τῆς ὀσμῆς τῆς εὐωδίας ὑμῶν ("I will not smell the aroma of your pleasing fragrance").

36. **"Appalled at it" (v. 32):** Hebrew וְשָׁמְמוּ עָלֶיהָ אֹיְבֵיכֶם, from שָׁמֵם ("to be desolate, to be appalled, to be stunned"). The same verb that means "to make desolate" (שַׁמּוֹתִי, v. 31) here describes the reaction of enemies who see the devastation — even conquerors are horrified by the scale of destruction. The LXX renders ἐκστήσονται ἐπ' αὐτῇ οἱ ἐχθροὶ ὑμῶν ("your enemies shall be astonished over it").

37. **"Unsheathe the sword after you" (v. 33):** Hebrew וַהֲרִיקֹתִי אַחֲרֵיכֶם חָרֶב, the Hiphil of רִיק ("to empty, to draw out") — "I will draw/empty out the sword behind you." The sword pursues the dispersed exiles even in their flight. The image of the drawn sword "after" a fleeing people captures relentless divine judgment that follows even in exile. The LXX renders ἐκκενώσω εἰς ὑμᾶς ῥομφαίαν ("I will empty/pour out the sword upon you").

38. **"The land shall enjoy its Sabbaths" (vv. 34, 35, 43):** Hebrew אֶת-שַׁבְּתֹתֶיהָ תִּרְצֶה הָאָרֶץ, lit. "the land shall be satisfied/pleased with its Sabbaths." The verb רָצָה means "to be pleased, to find favor in, to be satisfied with." The land denied its Sabbath rest by Israel's continuous cultivation (the Sabbath-year violations) will finally "enjoy" (be satisfied with) the forced rest of desolation. The LXX renders ἀναπαύσεται ἡ γῆ καὶ εὐδοκήσει τὰ σάββατα αὐτῆς ("the land shall rest and be pleased with its Sabbaths").

39. **"Rot away in your enemies' lands" (v. 39):** Hebrew יִמַּקּוּ בַּעֲוֺנָם. The verb מָקַק ("to waste away, to dissolve, to rot") in the Niphal suggests a gradual, organic decay — not violent destruction but slow dissolution. The image of the exile rotting away is striking: exile is not merely relocation but progressive disintegration, both physical and covenantal. The LXX renders τακήσονται ("they shall melt away/dissolve").

40. **"Their uncircumcised heart is humbled" (v. 41):** Hebrew יִכָּנַע לְבָבָם הֶעָרֵל. The "uncircumcised heart" (לֵב עָרֵל) is one of the most powerful figures in Hebrew anthropology — the heart that has not been opened, cut, made receptive to covenant relationship (cf. Deut 10:16; 30:6; Jer 4:4; Ezek 44:7). The humbling (כָּנַע, Niphal, "to be subdued/humbled") of the uncircumcised heart is the inner transformation that makes return and reconciliation possible. The LXX renders αἰσχυνθῶσιν ἐν τῇ ἀπεριτμήτῳ καρδίᾳ αὐτῶν ("they are ashamed in their uncircumcised heart").

41. **"Make amends for their iniquity" (vv. 41, 43):** Hebrew וְאָז יִרְצוּ אֶת-עֲוֺנָם, lit. "and then they will accept/pay for their iniquity." The verb רָצָה (here Qal, "to be pleased with, to accept, to bear") used reflexively or with עָוֺן as object means bearing the penalty of iniquity to satisfaction — the iniquity is "paid off" by the suffering endured in exile. The concept is penal and substitutionary: the exile serves as the satisfaction of the covenant debt. The LXX renders δέξωνται τὰς ἁμαρτίας αὐτῶν ("they shall receive/accept their sins").

42. **"I will remember my covenant with Jacob... Isaac... Abraham" (v. 42):** Hebrew וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי יַעֲקוֹב... יִצְחָק... אַבְרָהָם. The three patriarchal names appear in reverse chronological order — Jacob, Isaac, Abraham — a unique and intentional inversion. Commentators suggest this reversal emphasizes God's sovereign initiative: he works from the most recent covenant partner back to the foundational one, or it is a literary device emphasizing completeness and deliberateness. The LXX also preserves the reversed order. LOGOS translates literally without reordering.

43. **"The covenant with their forefathers" (v. 45):** Hebrew בְּרִית רִאשֹׁנִים, lit. "covenant of the first ones/ancestors." The term רִאשֹׁנִים ("the former ones, the first ones") here means the ancestral generation — the Exodus generation — rather than the patriarchs of v. 42. This is the Sinaitic covenant viewed from the perspective of its foundational moment in the sight of the nations. The LXX renders διαθήκης τῶν προτέρων ("covenant of the former ones").

44. **"In the sight of the nations, that I might be their God" (v. 45):** Hebrew לְעֵינֵי הַגּוֹיִם לִהְיֹת לָהֶם לֵאלֹהִים. The public, visible, international character of the Exodus is recalled as the ground of God's faithfulness in exile: just as the deliverance was performed openly before the nations, so the restoration will be. The covenant purpose — "that I might be their God" — frames the entire blessings-curses section as ultimately redemptive, not merely punitive. The LXX renders ἐναντίον τῶν ἐθνῶν εἶναι αὐτῶν θεός ("to be their God before the nations").

45. **"These are the statutes and rules and laws" (v. 46):** Hebrew אֵלֶּה הַחֻקִּים וְהַמִּשְׁפָּטִים וְהַתּוֹרֹת. Three terms: חֻקִּים ("statutes," the prescribed regulations without stated rationale), מִשְׁפָּטִים ("rules," the case-law judgments), and תּוֹרֹת ("laws/instructions," the plural of תּוֹרָה). The triple formulation is a summary and colophon to the Levitical legislation that runs from at least chapter 17 (the Holiness Code, chapters 17–26). The concluding phrase "on Mount Sinai through Moses" (בְּסִינַי בְּיַד-מֹשֶׁה) tethers the entire legal corpus to the Mosaic covenant. The LXX renders οὗτοι οἱ νόμοι καὶ τὰ κρίματα καὶ ὁ νόμος ("these are the laws and judgments and the law").

46. **Five-Stage Escalation Structure:** The covenant-curses section (vv. 14–39) is organized into five progressively intensifying judgment stages, each introduced by a conditional "if you will not listen/will not do" and each concluding with the sevenfold formula:
    - Stage 1 (vv. 14–17): Panic, disease, crop failure, military defeat
    - Stage 2 (vv. 18–20): Intensification; drought, iron sky, bronze earth
    - Stage 3 (vv. 21–22): Wild beasts, population collapse, deserted roads
    - Stage 4 (vv. 23–26): Sword, pestilence, siege, famine
    - Stage 5 (vv. 27–33): Cannibalism, destruction of cult sites, exile, desolation
    The structure mirrors ancient Near Eastern covenant-treaty curse lists (Hittite, Assyrian) but uniquely incorporates the theme of the land's Sabbath as both the violated blessing and the enforced penalty (vv. 34–35, 43).

47. **LXX Significant Variants:**
    - V. 1: LXX adds a prohibition against στήλη ("stele") more explicitly as a cultic standing stone than the MT's equivalent, slightly expanding the anti-idolatry list.
    - V. 4: LXX renders "the trees of the field shall yield their fruit" but adds ὁ ξύλον τοῦ παραδείσου ("the tree of the paradise/orchard"), a term that evokes Eden and is not in the MT.
    - V. 8: LXX renders "five shall chase a hundred" with πέντε ἑκατόν — exactly as MT — and "hundred shall chase ten thousand" with ἑκατὸν μυρίους, preserving the asymmetry (5→100 and 100→10,000, not 5→100 and 100→2,000 which the strict proportion would require).
    - V. 9: LXX adds ἐπιβλέψω ἐφ' ὑμᾶς ("I will look upon you") before "make you fruitful" — a plus not in the MT, adding a divine attentiveness clause.
    - V. 19: LXX renders "iron" for "heaven" and "bronze" for "earth" — same order as MT, unlike Deut 28:23 where LXX follows the MT reversal.
    - V. 30: LXX reads τὰ εἴδωλα ὑμῶν ("your idols") for both חַמָּנֵיכֶם and גִּלּוּלֵיכֶם, collapsing the MT's two distinct terms for pagan cult objects into one.
    - V. 34: LXX adds a significant expansion: the desolate land "shall be pleased" (εὐδοκήσει) with its Sabbaths — more explicit than the MT's רָצָה, which the LXX interprets as active satisfaction rather than mere rest.
    - V. 42: LXX preserves the reversed order Jacob→Isaac→Abraham and adds the phrase "and I will remember the land" as a separate clause, giving the land its own place alongside the three covenant partners.
    - V. 46: LXX renders τόν νόμον (singular) for the final תּוֹרֹת (plural), a significant shift that may reflect later LXX theological tendency to unify the Mosaic law under the singular νόμος.
